title: The Myth of Chinese Birth Tourism
gist: Chinese birth tourism is a tiny, Beijing-opposed industry recast as a US national-security threat, while the same China Brief flags Xi's continuing military purges, a second-strike missile test, and a Chinese AI compute crunch driving cloud prices up over 430%.

insights.claim: At its 2015 peak, birth tourism represented less than 0.3 percent of all US births, and recent annual cases (7,000-9,000) are overwhelmingly children of Hispanic mothers, mostly from Mexico.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Data-grounded debunking of a politicized narrative—useful for filtering signal from immigration-policy noise.

insights.claim: Births to nonresident Chinese mothers in the US number in the low hundreds each year, and Chinese agencies charge $36,000-$100,000 for packages, restricting it to the wealthy.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Quantifies how marginal the actual Chinese birth-tourism industry is.

insights.claim: Beijing explicitly opposes birth tourism because for wealthy Chinese families US citizenship is an insurance policy against a state that can arbitrarily imprison them or destroy their businesses.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Reframes the phenomenon as capital/elite flight from China, not a government infiltration plot.

insights.claim: Xi promoted Zhang Shuguang—now head of the Central Military Commission's Discipline Inspection Commission—and Wang Gang, positioning loyalists to continue purges before a new CMC is announced next year.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Ongoing PLA purges signal leadership instability and consolidation ahead of the 2027 CMC reshuffle.

insights.claim: China conducted a long-range missile test in the Pacific primarily to demonstrate its submarine-based second-strike nuclear capability to the US, alarming the Solomon Islands.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Advancing Chinese sea-based deterrence is a strategic-stability data point for the US-China balance.

insights.claim: China's surge in AI adoption is straining compute resources, with at least one firm raising prices for cloud services and processing power by more than 430 percent.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: A sharp compute-cost signal for Chinese AI—bullish for domestic chip/datacenter demand, bearish for AI-app margins.

insights.claim: The average Chinese AI query now demands five times as many tokens as a year ago, intensifying compute strain and the industry's price wars.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Token intensity is compounding compute demand—key driver of the China AI capex and pricing cycle.

insights.claim: Venture capital keeps pouring into China's money-losing AI sector on the belief that eventual winners will dominate a trillion-dollar industry.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Confirms a China AI bubble/land-grab dynamic akin to the US—watch for consolidation and capital burn.
